message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 29
Recent Green phase: Added validation that a quote with an unknown item type throws. In runScenario's handleQuote, a for-loop checks each item: if BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] === undefined, throw new Error(`Unknown item type: ${item.type}`).

Refactor while keeping all 29 tests green. Evaluate naming first, then Four Rules of Simple Design, then APP mass. Consider extracting the unknown-type validation into a well-named helper (e.g. assertKnownItemType or validateItems) for clarity, especially since more validation is coming (claim error cases).

IMPORTANT upcoming-test context (do NOT implement, keep design open): remaining error-case tests are (a) claim referencing an item not in the policy (wrong type, e.g. amulet damaged when only a sword is insured) → throw; (b) claim with more damage entries of a type than insured (two sword damages, one sword insured) → throw; (c) negative damage amount → throw. These suggest a validation seam for claims paralleling the quote validation. Do not build them yet. Keep all tests green.

Run autonomously, return after completion.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 29 tests green. Now let me proceed with the refactoring process.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The Green phase added an inline validation loop in `handleQuote`. Let me evaluate the naming and structure.

**Current state of the new code** (lines 188-201): `handleQuote` mixes three concerns:
1. validating items have known types
2. registering the policy
3. computing the premium

The inline `for` loop with `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] === undefined` does not reveal its intent at the call site. A reader must parse the loop body to learn "this rejects unknown item types."

**Naming decision**: Extract the validation into a well-named helper `assertKnownItemTypes`. This reveals intent (Rule 2) and — as the prompt notes — establishes a validation seam paralleling the coming claim-validation cases. I'll keep the design open: a single focused helper, not a premature `validateItems` umbrella.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass (the inline validation in `handleQuote`)

```
for (const item of step.items) {
  if (BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] === undefined) {
    throw new Error(`Unknown item type: ${item.type}`);
  }
}
```

Component count:
- Bindings: `item`, `step.items`, `item.type`, `item.type` = 4 → 4
- Constants: `undefined`, template string = 2 → 2
- Invocations: `BASE_PREMIUM[...]` index access, `throw new Error(...)` = 2 → 4
- Conditionals: 1 (`if`) → 4
- Loops: 1 (`for...of`) → 5

**Inline-validation mass: 19**, embedded inside `handleQuote`.

## Step 3 & 4: Apply Simple Design Rules — Extract `assertKnownItemTypes`

- **Rule 1 (Tests pass)**: green now; I'll re-run after.
- **Rule 2 (Reveals intent)**: extracting names the operation. The call site becomes `assertKnownItemTypes(step.items)` — self-documenting.
- **Rule 3 (No duplication)**: the existing module already uses a functional `.find`/`.some` style; using `.find` here matches the established idiom (consistency with `findInsuredItem`).
- **Rule 4 (Fewest elements)**: a `.find` expression replaces the explicit loop + counter-free mutation-free body.

Let me make the change. I'll place the helper near the other item predicates, after `BASE_PREMIUM` is defined.

input.old_string: const sum = (amounts: number[]): number => amounts.reduce((acc, amount) => acc + amount, 0);

content.text:
All 29 tests still green.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**At the call site** (`handleQuote`), the validation is now one invocation:
```
assertKnownItemTypes(step.items);
```
- Bindings: `step.items` = 1 → 1
- Invocations: 1 → 2

**Call-site mass: 3** (down from 19 inline).

**New helpers** (extracted, named, reusable):

`isKnownItemType`:
- Bindings: `type`, `type` = 2 → 2
- Constants: `undefined` = 1 → 1
- Invocations: `BASE_PREMIUM[type]` = 1 → 2
- Mass: 5

`assertKnownItemTypes`:
- Bindings: `items`, `unknown`, `item`, `item.type`, `unknown`, `unknown.type` = 6 → 6
- Constants: `undefined`, template string = 2 → 2
- Invocations: `.find`, `isKnownItemType`, `throw new Error` = 3 → 6
- Conditionals: 1 (`if`) → 4
- Loops: `.find` is iteration = 1 → 5
- Mass: 23

**Mass summary**:
- Before: 19, all concentrated inside `handleQuote` (mixed with policy-registration and premium concerns).
- After: 3 at the call site + 5 + 23 = 31 total across two named, single-purpose, reusable helpers.

The total mass rose (19 → 31), but this is the case the playbook flags explicitly: **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP**. The increase is the cost of replacing the explicit `for`/`if` loop with a `.find`-based predicate plus a named, intention-revealing seam. `handleQuote` is now markedly clearer (its validation concern reads as one named line), and `isKnownItemType` is a reusable predicate the upcoming claim-validation cases can build on without duplicating the `BASE_PREMIUM[...] !== undefined` knowledge.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: Extracted the inline unknown-type loop into `assertKnownItemTypes(items)` and a supporting predicate `isKnownItemType(type)`. The call site in `handleQuote` now reads as a single intention-revealing line.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): primary win — `handleQuote` no longer mixes validation mechanics with policy registration; the "reject unknown item types" rule is named.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): the `BASE_PREMIUM[...] !== undefined` knowledge now lives in one predicate (`isKnownItemType`), matching the module's existing `.find`-based idiom (`findInsuredItem`) and giving the coming claim-validation cases a seam to reuse rather than copy.
- APP: call-site mass 19 → 3; total mass 19 → 31. Accepted per "Rule 2 trumps APP" — the clarity and reuse gains justify the modest total increase.
- Design kept open: a single focused validator, not a premature `validateItems` umbrella, so claim-side validation can be added as its own parallel seam later.

## Step 7: Report

🔄 Refactoring Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted the inline unknown-item-type validation from `handleQuote` into two named helpers — `isKnownItemType(type)` (predicate) and `assertKnownItemTypes(items)` (guard) — placed near the other item predicates.
- **Mass Change**: call site 19 → 3; total 19 → 31 (increase accepted under "Rule 2 trumps APP").
- **Tests**: All 29 passing ✅

Files touched:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_04-34-58_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

The refactoring establishes a clean, named validation seam in the quote path that parallels the upcoming claim error-case tests (item-not-in-policy, excess damage entries, negative amounts) without pre-building them — keeping the design open while improving clarity.
